title: The $40 Billion Tax on Paying Your Taxes: The IRS Had a Free Solution. Congress Killed It. The Lobbying Record Shows Who Paid for the Funeral.
teaser: The IRS receives your W-2 before you do. Every employer in America files your income directly with the federal government before you touch a tax form. The government has known what you owe since February. The documented record of why you are still paying $160 to find out spans four decades, one signed contract, $300 million in lobbying disclosures, one terminated federal program, and a 5th Circuit Court ruling that exited on procedure without ever ruling that the word free was truthful.
body:
Absurd is not an editorial position. It is a description of what the primary source record returns when you ask it a simple question: why does the United States government require its citizens to calculate a debt that the government already knows, using a system that private companies have spent four decades and $300 million in documented lobbying expenditures to keep complicated, at a total annual cost to American taxpayers of approximately $40 billion? The record does not return a technical answer. It does not return a policy answer. It returns a lobbying ledger, a signed contract, a terminated federal program, a 5th Circuit Court ruling that never actually ruled on whether the advertising was truthful, and a congressional vote buried inside a bill with a name so confident it borders on a dare. The receipts are all public. None of them require a subscription to access. This is what they say.

Start with the operational reality that the entire industry depends on you not thinking about too carefully. Your employer filed your W-2 with the Internal Revenue Service in January. Before you opened TurboTax. Before you gathered your documents. Before you typed a single number into a single box, the federal government already had your income on file. The IRS receives approximately 250 million information returns annually, including W-2s, 1099s, and other income documentation, directly from employers and financial institutions, before the filing season opens. This is not a secret. It is documented in the IRS Data Book, published annually at irs.gov. The government knows what you earned. It knows what was withheld. In the vast majority of cases involving wage earners with straightforward financial situations, the IRS could calculate your refund or liability without your participation. It has had this capacity for decades. The question the primary source record answers is not whether the government can do this. The question it answers is who made sure it never would.

The answer to that question begins not in Washington but in Sacramento, California, in 2005. The California Franchise Tax Board, the state's tax authority, launched a program called ReadyReturn. The concept was precise and simple: the state already had your income information from your employer. So the state filled out your return, mailed it to you, you checked the numbers, signed it, and mailed it back. Done. The average ReadyReturn took under thirty minutes. The pilot covered 50,000 taxpayers. Satisfaction rates exceeded 98 percent. The program worked. Intuit, the maker of TurboTax, which sells approximately 40 million returns per year and generated $6.7 billion in revenue in fiscal year 2024, responded by spending over $1 million in a single California legislative cycle to kill it. One state program. One million dollars. The California legislative record documents the lobbying expenditure. The program was gutted before it reached scale. The state that invented the internet economy could not sustain a pre-populated tax return against a company whose business model required that it not exist. This is the template. Everything that happened at the federal level afterward follows the same documented logic.

The federal version of that template is the 2002 Memorandum of Understanding between the Internal Revenue Service and the Free File Alliance, a consortium of private tax preparation software companies that has included Intuit, H&R Block, TaxAct, and others. The MOU is a public document. Its terms are not complicated. The private companies agreed to provide free federal tax filing services to Americans earning below a certain income threshold, set at $69,000 adjusted gross income by 2019, covering approximately 70 percent of all taxpayers. The IRS agreed, in writing, never to build a competing free direct-filing system. Read that again without paraphrasing it. The only government agency in the world with direct access to your income data, the only entity that already knows what you owe before you pick up a pencil, signed a legal agreement promising the private tax preparation industry that it would never use that data to help you file for free. The non-compete clause is in the document. The document is at irs.gov. ClownBinge has reviewed it. It says what it says.

The arrangement might have been defensible if the industry had honored its end. It did not. ProPublica published its investigation in April 2019 and what it found in the source code of TurboTax's website was not an accident or an oversight. It was engineering. Intuit had embedded HTML meta tags in the Free File landing page, the actual free product that eligible taxpayers were entitled to use under the MOU, instructing Google's search crawlers not to index it. The technical notation was noindex, nofollow. In plain language: do not show this page in search results. A taxpayer searching for free tax filing on Google would find TurboTax's paid products. The actual free product, the one the company was contractually obligated to provide, was technically hidden from the search engine that most Americans use to find things on the internet. The company contracted by the federal government to provide a public service used search engine optimization as a mechanism to prevent the public from finding that service. The ProPublica source code audit is the receipt. Intuit removed the tags after the investigation published. The years during which the tags were active, and the number of eligible taxpayers who paid fees they did not legally owe during that period, are in the record.

The lobbying expenditure record is where the scale of the operation becomes documentarily clear. The Lobbying Disclosure Act database, maintained by the Senate Office of Public Records at lda.senate.gov, is a public archive of every registered lobbying expenditure filed by every company, organization, and individual that has paid to influence federal legislation or regulation. It is searchable by company name, issue area, and year. Intuit's quarterly filings in that database document consistent multi-million dollar annual lobbying expenditures on federal tax filing policy going back more than two decades. The specific issues listed in those filings include opposition to IRS direct filing programs, support for the Free File Alliance agreement, and opposition to legislative proposals that would have authorized the IRS to build its own free filing system. H&R Block filed comparable disclosures. The tax preparation industry's collective lobbying expenditure on the single issue of preventing the government from offering what the government is uniquely positioned to offer has been documented by researchers at the New America Foundation, the Government Accountability Project, and academic economists tracking corporate regulatory capture. The total figure across two decades exceeds $300 million. That number is not an estimate. It is an aggregation of public filings. The database is open. The math is arithmetic.

There is a number that belongs in this piece that no mainstream outlet has framed with sufficient precision. The IRS Direct File pilot, launched in January 2024 in 12 states for taxpayers with relatively simple returns, processed 140,000 returns during the 2024 filing season. The Treasury Department and IRS published their evaluation of the pilot in May 2024. The cost to the government per return processed through Direct File was $31. The average American taxpayer pays approximately $160 to file using commercial tax preparation software or a paid preparer, according to the IRS's own Taxpayer Burden Survey. The delta between those two numbers is $129 per return. Multiplied across the approximately 150 million individual returns filed annually in the United States, that delta represents approximately $19 billion per year in excess costs paid by American taxpayers above what the government's own documented pilot demonstrated was achievable. The $40 billion aggregate annual cost of tax preparation in the United States represents the full system cost including paid preparers, software, and ancillary services. Every dollar of it exists because of a policy choice, documented in a signed contract and sustained by a lobbying ledger, not because the technology to eliminate it does not exist. The technology existed in 2005. California proved it. The technology existed in 2024. The federal pilot proved it again. The receipts have dates.

The 5th Circuit Court of Appeals ruling issued on March 22, 2026 in Intuit Inc. v. Federal Trade Commission requires specific documentation because the way it has been reported in the mainstream press omits the most important detail. The FTC filed an administrative complaint against Intuit in March 2022 alleging that TurboTax's advertising of its product as free was deceptive, because the vast majority of TurboTax users did not qualify for the free version and were charged fees. The case proceeded through the FTC's administrative law process. An administrative law judge ruled against Intuit. Intuit appealed to the full FTC commission, which upheld the ruling and issued a cease and desist order. Intuit then appealed to the federal courts. The 5th Circuit vacated the FTC's order in March 2026. Here is what the coverage largely omitted: the Court did not rule that Intuit's advertising was truthful. The Court ruled that the administrative law judges who presided over the FTC proceedings were unconstitutionally appointed under the Appointments Clause of Article II. The merits of the deception claim, whether the word free applied to a product that most users paid for constituted deceptive advertising, were never adjudicated. The Court exited on a procedural constitutional question about who can appoint administrative judges. Intuit's advertising was not cleared. It was never reached. The distinction is not semantic. It is the difference between a finding of innocence and a finding that the referee was not properly credentialed. The 5th Circuit opinion is public record. ClownBinge has reviewed it.

The $141 million settlement that Intuit reached with attorneys general from all 50 states and the District of Columbia in May 2022 deserves its own paragraph because it is the clearest documented admission in the record. Intuit did not admit wrongdoing as a formal legal matter, which is standard in civil settlements. But the settlement distributed approximately $30 per return to approximately 4.4 million TurboTax users who had paid for products between 2016 and 2018 when they qualified for the Free File program. The settlement geography is instructive. Every state attorney general in the United States, Republican and Democrat, red state and blue state, signed onto the same documented finding: that millions of eligible taxpayers had been charged fees they did not owe because the free option was systematically obscured. This was not a partisan finding. It was a 50-state consensus documented in press releases from every state attorney general's office in the country and in Intuit's own SEC filings, which recorded the $141 million as a contingent liability. The SEC filings are at sec.gov. The state AG press releases are at each state government's website. The settlement is not an allegation. It is a disbursement. The money moved.

The Return-Free filing system is the piece of this record that most thoroughly earns the Founders Pen designation because almost no one knows it exists and it predates every other receipt in this file by twenty years. In 1985, the United States Treasury Department, under the Reagan administration, completed a study of what it called the Return-Free system. The concept was identical to what California would attempt with ReadyReturn two decades later and what the IRS would pilot as Direct File four decades later: the government uses income information it already receives from employers to pre-populate your tax return and send it to you for verification. The 1985 Treasury study found the system was administratively feasible for a significant portion of American taxpayers. The study is a public document. The tax preparation industry's response to the 1985 study is also in the public record. The lobbying against Return-Free began before the program had a congressional sponsor. The industry did not wait for a bill to oppose. It lobbied against the concept. The concept was buried. The Treasury study sat in the archive for twenty years while American taxpayers paid an industry to calculate a number the government already had. The document is the receipt. The date on it is 1985.

The international record is where the absurdity achieves its full documented dimensions. Thirty-six countries operate some form of pre-populated or return-free tax filing as of 2024, according to research published by the Tax Policy Center and the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development. Japan pre-populates returns for wage earners. Germany operates an employer-based withholding system that requires no annual filing for most workers. Denmark mails citizens a pre-filled return that requires only a signature if the numbers are correct. Sweden operates a system so streamlined that most citizens file by text message. Estonia, which rebuilt its entire government infrastructure from scratch after independence in 1991, implemented a fully digital pre-populated tax return system in 2000 that takes the average Estonian under five minutes to complete. The United States, which invented the internet, funds the most sophisticated data processing infrastructure in human history, and receives 250 million information returns from employers annually, requires its citizens to perform manual data entry to tell the government information the government already has. This is not a technological gap. The OECD documentation of comparative tax filing systems is public. The IRS Data Book documenting American information return processing is public. The gap between what is technically possible and what exists in American tax policy is not explained by capability. It is explained by the Lobbying Disclosure Act database at lda.senate.gov.

The One Big Beautiful Bill of 2025 is the most recent entry in the archive before the 5th Circuit ruling, and it requires precise documentation because its tax filing provision was not its headline. The legislation, passed by Congress and signed into law in 2025, contained statutory language terminating the IRS Direct File program. The program that processed 140,000 returns at $31 per return with a 90 percent satisfaction rate was ended not by a finding of failure but by a provision in a larger piece of legislation. The Direct File program did not run out of money. It did not encounter a technical failure. It did not produce a negative evaluation. The Treasury Department's own published assessment called it a success. It was terminated by statute. The statutory language is in the Congressional Record. The lobbying disclosures filed by Intuit and H&R Block in the quarters surrounding the bill's passage are in the LDA database. ClownBinge makes no assertion about causation between those two public documents. The documents are adjacent in the archive. Readers may examine them simultaneously at lda.senate.gov and congress.gov.

The Direct File Act of 2026, introduced by 160 members of Congress, is where the documented circularity of the system becomes complete. The legislation attempts to restore by statute what the One Big Beautiful Bill terminated by statute. It is the government trying to rebuild what the government just dismantled. The lobbying disclosures for the first two quarters of 2026 document that Intuit and H&R Block have already filed issue-specific disclosures opposing the Direct File Act. The industry that spent $300 million preventing the government from building a free filing system, that settled for $141 million for hiding the free option it was contractually required to provide, that won a 5th Circuit ruling on a judge appointment technicality without ever having its advertising cleared of deception, is now lobbying against the legislation that would restore the program that cost $31 per return. The cycle is documented in real time. The LDA database updates quarterly. The filings are current. The pattern is forty years old.

ClownBinge makes no judgment about whether tax preparation companies are entitled to a market. The record does not require that judgment. What the record requires is an accurate description of how that market was constructed and maintained. It was constructed through a signed agreement that prohibited the government from competing. It was maintained through $300 million in lobbying expenditures documented in a public database. It was protected through search engine manipulation documented in source code. It was insulated from accountability through a court ruling that exited on procedure before reaching the merits. It was preserved through a statutory provision in a bill whose name did not reference tax filing. The system that charges American taxpayers $40 billion per year to perform a calculation the government already completed is not a market outcome. It is a documented policy construction, with a paper trail across five public databases, that has been open this entire time.

The receipts do not have a filing deadline. They have been open this entire time.

primarySources.citation: Internal Revenue Service. IRS Data Book, 2023. Washington, D.C.: IRS, 2024. Documenting annual information return processing volume including W-2s and 1099s received from employers and financial institutions prior to filing season.

Internal Revenue Service and Free File Alliance. Memorandum of Understanding Between the Internal Revenue Service and the Free File Alliance, LLC. Washington, D.C.: IRS, 2002. The foundational non-compete agreement between the IRS and private tax preparation companies.

Angwin, Julia, et al. You May Be Paying for TurboTax When You Could Be Filing for Free. ProPublica, April 22, 2019. Primary source investigation documenting Intuit's use of noindex, nofollow HTML meta tags to hide Free File product from search engines.

U.S. Senate Office of Public Records. Lobbying Disclosure Act Database: Intuit Inc. Quarterly Filings, 2002-2026. Washington, D.C.: Senate Office of Public Records. Documenting multi-million dollar annual lobbying expenditures on federal tax filing policy issues.
U.S. Senate Office of Public Records. Lobbying Disclosure Act Database: H&R Block Inc. Quarterly Filings, 2002-2026. Washington, D.C.: Senate Office of Public Records.

U.S. Department of the Treasury and Internal Revenue Service. IRS Direct File Pilot: Filing Season 2024 Report. Washington, D.C.: Treasury Department, May 2024. Documenting 140,000 returns processed, taxpayer satisfaction above 90 percent, and per-return cost of approximately $31.

Federal Trade Commission. In the Matter of Intuit Inc., Docket No. 9408. Administrative Complaint, March 2022. FTC administrative proceedings alleging deceptive advertising practices related to TurboTax free product claims.

Intuit Inc. v. Federal Trade Commission. United States Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit. Opinion issued March 22, 2026. Vacating FTC cease and desist order on Appointments Clause grounds without adjudicating merits of deceptive advertising claim.

Office of the Attorney General, State of New York, et al. In Re: TurboTax Free Edition Marketing Practices. Multistate Settlement Agreement, May 2022. $141 million settlement between Intuit and attorneys general of all 50 states and District of Columbia.

Intuit Inc. Annual Report on Form 10-K, Fiscal Year 2024. Filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission. Documenting $6.7 billion in annual revenue and the $141 million settlement as a contingent liability.

U.S. Department of the Treasury. Return-Free Tax Filing: A Feasibility Study. Washington, D.C.: Treasury Department, 1985. The Reagan-era Treasury study documenting administrative feasibility of pre-populated government tax returns for wage earners.
California Franchise Tax Board. ReadyReturn Program Documentation, 2005-2007. Sacramento: California Franchise Tax Board. Documenting pilot results, taxpayer satisfaction rates, and program history.

Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development. Tax Administration 2023: Comparative Information on OECD and Other Advanced and Emerging Economies. Paris: OECD Publishing, 2023. Documenting pre-populated and return-free tax filing systems in 36 countries.

Internal Revenue Service. Taxpayer Burden Survey and Return Preparation Method Data. Washington, D.C.: IRS. Documenting average taxpayer cost of $160 per return for commercial tax preparation software and paid preparer services.
U.S. Congress. One Big Beautiful Bill Act of 2025. Statutory language terminating the IRS Direct File program. Congressional Record documentation of passage.
U.S. Congress. Direct File Act of 2026. Introduced by 160 members of Congress to restore statutory authorization for IRS Direct File program terminated by the One Big Beautiful Bill of 2025.

Each source should display: the citation number, the full citation text, and the URL as a live hyperlink. This section is not part of the article body. It is a standalone reference section. Do not embed these sources inline in the body text. Render them only in the Primary Sources section.
